title: Chapter 19: The Floodgates
content: Luna came back at 10:47 AM.

She emerged from the bathroom vent—of all places—dropping into the apartment with a grace that made me jealous, followed by a woman I'd never met. Maria was small, dark-haired, with the hollowed-out look of someone who'd spent too long being afraid. She was shaking, crying silently, and Luna was holding her hand with the same gentle firmness she used with me.
"Done," Luna said. "Derek didn't see. He's in the living room, watching the courtyard. He has headphones on. Music."
"Headphones?" I couldn't believe it. Derek, the paranoid survivalist, was wearing *headphones* while armed strangers approached his building?
"Whiskey," Luna explained, as if that answered everything. It kind of did.

Maria collapsed on the couch next to Jessica, and the two women looked at each other with the instant recognition of people who'd survived the same monster. No words necessary. Just a silent *I know. I understand. It's over now.*
"Phase Two," I said. "Everyone in position."

Mia was already on the roof—her sniper's nest, with a clear view of the building's approaches and seven rounds that needed to count. She'd stay up there, providing overwatch and picking off any Clan members who broke through.

Sarah and Jessica were in Apartment 12B, door sealed, Boss under the bed. Non-combatants, but not helpless—Sarah had the first aid kit and the tactical awareness to keep them alive if anything breached the perimeter.
I was in the stairwell on Floor 12, and Luna was one floor below me on Floor 11.
Our job: open the doors.

Every stairwell door on every floor had a push-bar release that could be operated from inside the stairwell. In normal times, these were fire exits—the building code required them to be easy to open from the inside. In apocalypse times, they were the keys to a trap that had been building for two weeks.

The zombies weren't moving because the doors were closed. Zombies didn't pull handles. They pushed, they battered, they broke glass—but the heavy fire doors of Crimson Ridge Complex were designed to resist exactly that kind of pressure. As long as the doors stayed closed, the zombies stayed on their floors, contained and dormant.
I was about to unlock every cage in the building.
"On my mark," I said, my hand on the Floor 11 push-bar. "Floors 1 through 6: open. Floors 7 through 10: open. Floor 8: stay closed—one floor between Derek and the horde is a mercy I'm not granting."
"Floor 8 stays closed," Luna confirmed from below. Her hands were on the Floor 10 bar.
"Floor 12: sealed. Our floor stays locked. Nothing comes up unless we let it."
"Understood."
I took a breath. In the silence of the stairwell, I could hear the Warehouse Clan below—footsteps on the ground floor, the crash of the main entrance being forced, shouted orders. They were in.
"Mark."

I pushed the bar. The door swung open with a pneumatic hiss, and the sound that followed was like nothing I'd ever heard—a wave of moaning, shuffling, *hunger* that rose from the lower floors as every dormant zombie in the building detected the same thing I had: open doors. Fresh prey. New territory.
Luna opened Floor 10. Then 9. Then 7.
And on the lower floors— Floors 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1—the zombies poured into the hallways.
From the roof, Mia narrated the chaos.
"They're in the building—ground floor, main entrance. Eight confirmed, moving in two-man teams. They've reached the stairwell—hold on."

A long pause. Then: "The dead are moving. They heard the doors. Jesus, there's... there must be thirty of them on the ground floor alone. The Clan's engaging—they've got the numbers, but the dead don't stop. One of them just took a machete to the chest and kept coming."
Another pause. Gunfire—Mia's rifle, cracking through the radio with compressed thunder.
"One down. Clan member, not zombie. He got separated from his group and a crawler got his leg. His friends aren't coming back for him."

The math was doing itself. The Clan had entered the building expecting to fight a few survivors and loot an apartment. Instead, they'd walked into a building that was a vertical maze of the dead—zombies on every floor, pouring out of stairwells and hallways, attacking anything that moved. The Clan's numbers and discipline were meaningless against enemies that didn't feel pain, didn't fear death, and didn't stop.
"They're retreating," Mia reported. "Three of them made it to the second floor, but the zombies followed. Four more are pinned in the ground-floor lobby. I can see Derek—"
My heart stopped. "Derek?"
"He came out of 8A. He's on the stairwell landing, looking down at the chaos. He sees what's happening. He's... he's going back inside. Locking his door."

Of course he was. Derek Voss, the man who'd brought an army to kill us, was retreating into his apartment and letting that army die. It was the most Derek thing imaginable—use people, then abandon them when they stopped being useful.
"Clan status?" I asked.
"Two confirmed dead on the ground floor. Four fighting to reach the exit. Two made it outside—I can see them running. They're gone. The building is..."
Another pause. Longer this time.
"The building is clear of living hostiles. Zombies are feeding on the ground floor. The stairwell between floors 1 and 4 is... I can't describe it. It's bad."

The trap had worked. The zombies had done what zombies do—fill every available space, overwhelm through numbers, and leave nothing but the dead behind them. The Warehouse Clan was broken, scattered, destroyed by the very building they'd come to raid.
And somewhere on Floor 8, behind a locked door, Derek Voss was waiting for us to come for him.

**QUEST UPDATE: DEFEND YOUR FAMILY**
**TIER 1 (Survive 24 hours): IN PROGRESS — 14 hours remaining**
**TIER 2 (Neutralize all hostile combatants): 2 of 10 remaining (Derek Voss, Rick Torres)**
**TIER 3 (Zero casualties among allies): INTACT**

Two hostiles left. Derek and Rick. The Clan was shattered, the building was a charnel house, and the only thing standing between us and the titanium safe house was one man with a gun and his frightened flunky.

I looked at Luna. She was standing in the stairwell doorway, her axe resting on her shoulder, blood on her hands—zombie blood, black and congealed, from clearing the stragglers on Floor 11. Her expression was calm.
"Floor 8?" she asked.
"Floor 8," I confirmed.
"Derek?"
"Derek."
She shifted her grip on the axe. Adjusted her stance. And for the first time since I'd known her, I saw something in her eyes that wasn't calmness.
It was anticipation.
"Good," she said. "I have words for him."
I had a feeling "words" was a euphemism for something significantly more violent. And for once, I was okay with that.
Some people needed more than words.
Some people needed the axe.
